agent.root_goal: Produce a bilingual (EN/JP) developer style guide for 'Yuten', an internal genomics analysis platform, in two independently-owned parts. Part A (pipeline standards team): first survey the naming actually used by Yuten's three legacy pipelines (aligner, variant-caller, annotator), recording every legacy stage name, file-format tag, and quality-flag code in use; then decide and write the canonical terminology standard (stage nouns, file-format vocabulary, quality-flag casing rules). The published standard lists ONLY the new canonical terms, not the legacy ones. Part B (scientific documentation team): write the worked pipeline tutorial with runnable command-line samples, covering four sections in both languages: the alignment step, the variant-calling step, quality filtering, and migration notes for labs upgrading from the legacy pipelines - the migration notes must map each legacy stage name and flag code to its canonical replacement, old -> new. Lab handbook rule, applying to the whole guide: documentation ships only if every stage name, file-format tag, and quality-flag code appearing anywhere in the guide matches the published canonical standard exactly; term drift between parts is a shipping defect.

action.reasoning: The root task requires producing a bilingual developer style guide split into two parts with a strict dependency: Part B (tutorial and migration notes) cannot be written correctly until Part A (canonical terminology standard) is finalized, because the lab handbook rule forbids term drift. I will spawn two subtasks. Subtask 1 (Part A) will survey the legacy pipelines and produce the canonical standard. Subtask 2 (Part B) will be gated on the completion of Subtask 1, ensuring it uses the exact canonical terms for the tutorial and migration mapping. I will take a self_role to perform a final integration review to ensure consistency across both parts before the guide is considered complete.

subtasks.goal: Survey Yuten's three legacy pipelines and produce the canonical terminology standard.
subtasks.capsule: Act as the pipeline standards team. First, simulate or recall the naming conventions used by Yuten's three legacy pipelines (aligner, variant-caller, annotator), recording every legacy stage name, file-format tag, and quality-flag code. Then, decide on and write the canonical terminology standard. The published standard must list ONLY the new canonical terms (stage nouns, file-format vocabulary, quality-flag casing rules), not the legacy ones. This output is the single source of truth for terminology.

outputs.description: A comprehensive list of all legacy stage names, file-format tags, and quality-flag codes found in the aligner, variant-caller, and annotator pipelines.
The official canonical terminology standard for Yuten, including stage nouns, file-format vocabulary, and quality-flag casing rules. Contains only new canonical terms.

subtasks.goal: Write the bilingual worked pipeline tutorial and migration notes using the canonical standard.
subtasks.capsule: Act as the scientific documentation team. Write a worked pipeline tutorial with runnable command-line samples in both English and Japanese. Cover four sections: alignment step, variant-calling step, quality filtering, and migration notes. The migration notes must map each legacy stage name and flag code (from root.1/legacy_survey) to its canonical replacement (from root.1/canonical_standard). Every term used must match the canonical standard exactly to avoid term drift.

outputs.description: The English version of the worked pipeline tutorial, including alignment, variant-calling, quality filtering, and migration notes.
The Japanese version of the worked pipeline tutorial, including alignment, variant-calling, quality filtering, and migration notes.

self_role.goal: Perform final integration review to ensure terminology consistency across the entire guide.

outputs.description: A review report confirming that all terms in the tutorial (root.2) match the canonical standard (root.1) exactly, and that no term drift exists.

These are logically sequential (survey informs standard) but can be handled efficiently by a single agent or split into two subtasks.
